channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A $3.2 Trillion Deal-Making Frenzy Is Spurred by the A.I. Economy
item.description: This year’s boom includes the most spent on global deal-making in a six-month period in a decade. But questions persist about whether it can continue.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The frenzy has heavily favored large companies, with 44 deals announced in the first six months of this year that were larger than $10 billion.

item.title: Grocery Stores Lower Prices as Consumers Pare Spending
item.description: While shoppers may get better deals on some items, it’s unlikely their overall grocery bill will fall.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell and Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Consumers are making fewer trips to traditional grocery stores in favor of discount chains like Aldi, which opened a store in Westlake, Fla., last year as part of its U.S. expansion.

item.title: Erika Kirk Requests All Evidence in Husband’s Killing to Be Shown Publicly
item.description: A lawyer for Charlie Kirk’s family said disclosure would help quell conspiracy theories, but the judge ruled that not all evidence would be shown in the courtroom or on a livestream.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:description: Erika Kirk has become the object of conspiracy theories.

item.title: Graham Platner’s Exit Sets Off Scramble for New Democratic Senate Candidate in Maine
item.description: The state Democratic Party has said it will pick a replacement through a nominating convention before a July 27 deadline. Candidates are already lining up.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Graham Platner and Troy Jackson at a joint campaign rally at the Collins Center for the Arts on the University of Maine campus in May.

item.title: Inside the Implosion of Graham Platner’s Maine Senate Campaign: ‘A Slow-Rolling Disaster’
item.description: Graham Platner’s bid for the Senate inspired progressive Democrats. But the campaign, which he suspended Wednesday, was messy, disorganized and ultimately doomed by a steady drip of scandal.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Katie Glueck and Michael Kruse
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Maine Democrats Hope to Replace Platner as Senate Nominee. Has That Ever Worked?
item.description: Graham Platner is one of just a handful of candidates in recent decades who have withdrawn from a Senate race after securing a major party nomination.
item.dc:creator: C.J. Robinson
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Nam Y. Huh
item.media:description: Alan Keyes, right, was a replacement Senate nominee in Illinois in 2004 after the winner of the Republican primary withdrew amid scandal. He lost to Barack Obama by 43 points.

item.title: Tanker Attacks Risk Overplaying Iran’s Hand, Analysts Say
item.description: Iran’s history shows it is willing to dig in, even when it may suffer big losses.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels on the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday. Traffic last weekend was about one-third of the prewar level of more than 100 ships daily, split evenly between the Iranian and Omani sides of the waterway.

item.title: Too Many Books?
item.description: Mendel Uminer faced a crisis when his landlord objected to the 10,000 volumes in his New York studio apartment.
item.dc:creator: Alex Vadukul
item.media:description: “I don’t think of myself as a hoarder,” Mendel Uminer said, “but I guess my building did.”

item.title: Ukrainians Cautiously Welcome Trump’s Announcement About Patriot Missile Systems
item.description: President Trump said the United States would license Ukraine to produce Patriots, which can intercept ballistic missiles. But it could be months or years before those are ready.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Oleksandr Chubko
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine meeting with President Trump on the sidelines of the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: For Ukraine, Other Patriot Makers Are a Cautionary Tale
item.description: Two American allies, Germany and Japan, already have permission to build the American interceptors, a license that President Trump says he will also grant to Kyiv.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley, Javier C. Hernández and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Japan’s defense minister, Shinjiro Koizumi, second right, inspecting a Patriot missile interceptor unit in Tokyo in December.

item.title: Trump’s NATO Summit, and Everyone Else’s
item.description: While President Trump insulted allies and demanded loyalty, the military alliance moved quietly closer to accepting more European responsibility for defense.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.media:description: President Trump and the leaders of other NATO countries in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Gaza Aid Worker on His Way to Watch World Cup Killed by Israeli Strike
item.description: The Israeli military said it had struck a Hamas militant but was aware of reports that “uninvolved civilians” were harmed.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Abu Bakr Bashir
item.media:description: Mourners carrying the body of Mohammed al-Waheidi, an aid worker killed in an Israeli strike on Tuesday, during his funeral, in Gaza City, on Wednesday.

item.title: Palestinian Authority Sets Date for First Major Elections in 20 Years
item.description: Analysts expressed skepticism that the vote would take place as planned. In 2021, Mahmoud Abbas, the authority’s leader, scheduled and then canceled a similar election.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: Mahmoud Abbas, the president of the Palestinian Authority speaking during a conference in Ramallah, in the Israeli-occupied West Bank, in May. His term ended in 2009, but he has since canceled elections.

item.title: This Summer, Midtown Manhattan Is Taking Center Stage
item.description: Midtown, with its high-rise office buildings and tourist hotels, isn’t hip, but it has become the site of a series of dramatic spectacles this year.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Midtown Manhattan is in the heart of the borough, but many New Yorkers prefer to avoid it.

item.title: China, Russia and Others Seek to Inflame Debate Over A.I. Data Centers
item.description: State actors in China, Russia and Iran have sought to exploit the U.S. public debate over the effects of the technology.
item.dc:creator: Steven Lee Myers and Dustin Volz
item.media:description: OpenAI released this cartoon as an example of one generated with ChatGPT by people in China whom the company believed were affiliated with a regional government. It is not clear what word in the title was blurred out.

item.title: Big Tech Is Now Targeting Native American Land for Massive Data Centers
item.description: The data center boom has roiled communities across the country, but on Native land, a Big Tech push for quick approvals has pitted the need for development against a history of exploitation.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen and Tamir Kalifa
item.media:description: “True wealth is the well-being of our families,” Chebon Kernell, a tribal council member for the Seminole Nation, said during a tour of his family’s cemetery.

item.title: Wally Funk, Who Set an Age Record for Space Travel, Dies at 87
item.description: As a young woman in the 1960s, she wasn’t allowed to become a NASA astronaut. She finally realized her long-held dream of flying in space as an octogenarian.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein
item.media:description: Wally Funk in 2021, after returning from a roughly 10-minute spaceflight aboard a rocket built by the company Blue Origin. “I loved every minute of it,” she said. “I just wish it had been longer.”

item.title: Review: ‘Little House on the Prairie’ Adds On an Expansion
item.description: The classic story (and recent culture-war object) remains wholesome but makes room for people who called the prairie home before the Ingalls family.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)

item.title: A New ‘Little House’ Broadens and Updates the Prairie
item.description: A Netflix adaptation of the beloved tale empowers Ma and adds new Black and Native characters to challenge prejudices. The creators know criticism is coming.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)
Little House on the Prairie (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Karen Asher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Netflix’s “Little House on the Prairie” was filmed in Winnipeg. The new Ingalls family includes Skywalker Hughes as Mary, left, and Alice Halsey as Laura.

item.title: How ‘Little House’ Welcomes Native Neighbors
item.description: The real Ingalls family built their “little house on the prairie” on Osage land and evinced racist attitudes. A new TV adaptation tries to reckon with this while preserving the story’s heart.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)
Little House on the Prairie (TV Program)
item.media:description: From left, Xander Cole and Corvin Mack on horseback filming a new adaptation of “Little House on the Prairie” reshaped to include Native characters and perspectives.

item.title: ‘ I’m Worried About Weimar America’
item.description: “It may not be Hitler 2.0. It may not be Stalin 2.0. It might be something all American, but it’s not going to be what we’re used to,” the author Rod Dreher argues.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat, Rochelle Widdowson and Raina Raskin

item.title: ‘There’s No Graham Platner Without Donald Trump’: 3 Writers on the Fiasco in Maine
item.description: How much have the rules of politics changed?
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg, Alex Seitz-Wald and Matthew Yglesias
item.media:credit: Mark Peterson for The New York Times

item.title: Bonnie Tyler, Who Sang ‘Total Eclipse of the Heart,’ Dies at 75
item.description: With a voice both weathered and operatic, she minted 1980s pop anthems like “Total Eclipse” and “Holding Out for a Hero.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Adrian Greer Michael Short/Fairfax Media, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bonnie Tyler in 1983. Her biggest hit, “Total Eclipse of the Heart,” became a cultural mainstay that has 1.1 billion streams on Spotify and 1.3 billion views on YouTube.
